articles.name: How to Change your Recurring Gift on myFCA
articles.title: How to Change your Recurring Gift on myFCA
articles.body:
You can easily change your recurring gift to FCA through the myFCA Donor Login. Follow these instructions to learn how.

First, visit my.fca.org and click the "Manage My Donation" link in the upper right-hand corner:

On the next page, enter the email address you've used when making your donations and click the "Get Login Link" button:

You should then receive an email at that address with a special link to log in to your myFCA account. Simply click the link (the "Login" button) in that email to proceed:

FYI

If none of your email addresses are recognized, contact FCA Support Services at 1-800-289-0909 and they can help you reset the email address associated with your account.

Once you're logged in, you'll see your myFCA account with information about your gift(s):

To edit a gift, simply click the adjacent gear icon ( ):

Then on the next page, click "Edit Payment Details":

In the "Edit payment details" pop-up window, you can make changes to your gift. You can change the "Donation amount," you can opt to "Cover transaction costs," the gift "Frequency," the timing of the next installment, you can opt to "Make an extra installment now," and the "Payment method":

After making any changes, simply click the "Confirm" button. Your changes will be active immediately!

articles.name: Fundraising Boot Camp FAQs
articles.title: Fundraising Boot Camp FAQs
articles.body:
How do I register for a Boot Camp?

When your hiring manager advances you in the hiring process, you will receive an email to this page in FCA University where you can register for an upcoming Boot Camp at the FCA Support Center.

How much does Boot Camp cost?

Boot Camp costs $250 which is charged to your FCA ORG account, unless otherwise specified to the FCA Training Department.

Lodging

Where do we stay for Boot Camp?

You will be staying at the Drury Inn and Suites: 20300 East 42nd St South, Blue Springs, MO. You will be assigned a roommate from amongst your Boot Camp classmates. The price of the hotel is included in your $250 registration fee.

Can I have my own room?

As long as the hotel has space, you can request your own room at registration for an additional cost of $90 per night, charged to your FCA ORG.

Can my spouse come too?

Yes, we encourage spouses to attend Boot Camp! It's an additional $125 for your spouse to attend. This covers their materials, the hotel room, food, and transportation. Your spouse is free to participate in Boot Camp as much as they'd like to—whatever they feel comfortable with.

If your spouse wants to travel with you, but not participate in the Boot Camp, then you must simply cover the cost of your own hotel room, which is $90 per night.

Can a family member or friend travel with me to Boot Camp?

Yes, although they cannot participate in the Boot Camp. Again, the additional cost would simply be the hotel room ($90/night).

I was not asked for payment information when I registered. How do I pay?

The cost of Boot Camp will be debited from your FCA ORG account at the end of the Boot Camp. The FCA Training Department will initiate this transfer. Talk to your Hiring Manager about your FCA ORG and what may or may not be debited from that account.

Travel

How do I book my airfare?

Please plan your travel so that you arrive on the Monday immediately before your Boot Camp begins (Boot Camp always begins promptly on a Tuesday at 8:00 AM). Fly to Kansas City International Airport (MCI) before 6:00 PM. We will arrange a complimentary shuttle to take you from MCI to the Drury Inn and Suites. If you arrive after 6:00 PM on the Monday immediately before your Boot Camp, you will be charged for the cost of your shuttle to the hotel.

You should book your departure out of MCI for any time after 6:00 PM on Wednesday of the same week you arrive. We will arrange a shuttle to take you from the FCA Support Center to MCI. (Boot Camp concludes at 4:00 PM, so we must budget for travel to the airport—the FCA Support Center is about 35 minutes from MCI).

If you are unable to book a departure flight in that time frame, we will arrange for and cover the cost of 1 additional night at a different hotel room (closer to MCI). The hotel can provide a complimentary shuttle to the airport on Thursday (you will be responsible for arranging that shuttle with the hotel). You can book your departure flight for any time on that Thursday.

If you'd like help to book your airfare, you may use our travel agent by completing a simple form (click here). There is a $50 fee for booking through the agent; they can charge that fee to your FCA ORG.

Do I need to pay for my own flight?

Please talk to your Hiring Manager.

Can I drive to Kansas City?

Certainly! If you plan to drive simply go directly to the hotel on the Monday immediately before your Boot Camp begins. You may elect to arrive on the Tuesday morning that your Boot Camp begins (but keep in mind that there are no excused absences from any of the Boot Camp sessions). In that event, go straight to the FCA Support Center and not to the hotel. Again, the first session of Boot Camp begins promptly at 8:00 AM on Tuesday.

Boot Camp concludes at 4:00 PM on Wednesday, you may depart any time after that. As with flying, if you prefer to depart on Thursday, we will arrange for and cover the cost of 1 additional night at the hotel.

Attention

Whether you are flying or driving, please send your travel itinerary to training@fca.org as soon as possible so necessary arrangements can be made. If you haven't sent your travel itinerary at least 2 weeks prior to your Boot Camp, you will have to make your own arrangements.

Can I switch to a different Boot Camp date or change my travel itinerary?

Yes. But please email training@fca.org as soon as possible.

How will I get from the hotel to the FCA Support Center each day?

We will arrange for a shuttle to transport you each day.

Is there a dress code?

The dress code is business casual, which means no shorts, tank tops, or athletic gear. Nice jeans are permissible. (To put it this way, dress as if you were going to meet a potential donor to ask them to join your Home Team!)

What is the schedule for Boot Camp like?

The table below is an overview of a standard week at Boot Camp:

Monday Tuesday Wednesday Thursday
Arrive in Kansas City.

Full-day Boot Camp training at the FCA Support Center.

Meals provided.

Full-day Boot Camp training at the FCA Support Center.

Meals provided.

Depart Kansas City.

 Optional departure from Kansas City (if necessary).

FYI

You should receive a confirmation email after you register for Boot Camp in FCA University, as well as a general information email (like this one) a few days later.

If you do not receive these, please contact training@fca.org.

Hiring Managers

My new hire hasn't yet completed the necessary steps in the Hiring Process, (i.e., we are waiting on a background check, etc.) can he or she still register for Boot Camp?

A new hire may attend Boot Camp without a background check, but, there is a potential downside: if their eventual background check is not satisfactory, you would have spent money to send them to Boot Camp and may fail to ultimately hire them.

Similarly, if they have not signed a Letter of Intent, your new hire may nonetheless withdraw from the process for any reason.

In short, it is best to complete all the necessary prerequisites before proceeding to this step. Boot Camp represents a genuine investment in your candidate.

articles.name: Worship Leader Instructions
articles.title: Worship Leader Instructions
articles.body:
Coaches TimeOut (CTO) is the coaches marriage ministry of FCA. CTO helps coaches and their spouses grow in their relationships with Jesus Christ and each other.

As the CTO Worship Leader, you play a vital role in helping coaches and their spouses in that journey. Your purpose is to help usher coaches and their spouses into the presence of God during the event's main sessions.

Your leadership lays the groundwork for discipleship conversations during and after the main sessions. The intent of worshipping together during the main sessions is for coaches and spouses to prepare their hearts for the speaker's message, which will compel deeper discussion with one another (1-on-1) and in the context of community (Huddles).

You can help coaches and spouses win where it matters most—at home.

The outline below is intended to help you prepare to lead worship at the CTO event.

FYI

Make sure to have regular discussions with your CTO Director about the overall vision for the CTO event. And ask about your specific audience, so you can prepare accordingly.

Quick Tips

  • Variety is essential; mix familiar songs with new ones, upbeat songs with slow, solemn ones. A lack of variety may alienate some attendees.
  • Stick with songs with a simple melody and lyrics. Not all radio hits make good worship songs. They may sound great, but can oftentimes be too complex both lyrically and melodically.
  • Stay consistent. Review the Annual Ministry Theme resources and incorporate the theme in your sets. The Daily Themes may even help you choose your songs.
  • Stay engaged throughout the whole program. Attendees are more inclined to engage themselves when they are more familiar with you personally! If you connect with the coaches and their spouses offstage, they will connect with you onstage.
  • Organize and mobilize prayer. Enlist key people to pray for you, the emcee, the speaker, the main sessions, the one-on-ones, the coaches, and their spouses.
  • CTO is designed for married coaches and spouses of all ages and sports.
    • It is also designed for coaches and their spouses wherever they may be in their spiritual journey.
  • Your audience will inevitably consist of members of various church denominations

Gospel-Centered

The Fellowship of Christian Athletes is an inter-denominational discipleship ministry inviting coaches and athletes into a personal relationship with Jesus Christ. FCA is focused on communicating the basics of the Gospel message:

  • God loves you.
  • Sin separates you from God.
  • Jesus rescues you (the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ).
  • The opportunity to trust Jesus (Will you trust Jesus?).

Visit The FOUR

Since FCA is an inter-denominational ministry, please avoid potentially divisive topics that attend denominationalism, such as baptism, communion, and spiritual gifts (such as speaking in tongues, healing, and prophecy).

Curriculum and Resources

Faith, Family, Finance

To help coaches and their spouses grow in their relationships with Jesus Christ and with each other, a CTO event will focus on three main topics:

  1. Faith
    Regardless of where coaches and spouses are on their spiritual journeys, Jesus Christ has a greater plan for their marriage. When He's at the center, everything changes in their lives.
  2. Family
    Coaches are always on the go. Pretty soon, they may realize that they spend more time in the locker room than they do at the dinner table. When the scoreboard finally turns off, a coach’s spouse and family shouldn't settle for whatever's left—they can get coach’s best.
  3. Finance
    Whether coaches make a little or a lot, a lack of intentionality can cause financial pressure sure to increase the strain on their marriage. With a foundation of Biblical principles, coaches and spouses can steward their money in healthy ways.
Your primary focus should be on faith when you lead worship and speak during the main sessions. Please consult with your CTO Director if he or she would like you to address other topics.

CTO HANDBOOK

Based on FCA's annual ministry theme, the CTO Handbook guides coaches and their spouses on a journey to apply key Biblical truth to their marriage. Each lesson explores Biblical teaching on faith, family, and finance and provides couples with discussion questions to help them apply what they learned to their marriage.

CTO Handbook

Ideally, the Handbook can help create a rhythm for a coach and their spouse to have a “one-on-one” together, studying God’s Word and discussing its application to their lives, marriage, and coaching. At a CTO event, this resource may be used during these one-on-one times, at Huddles, or even as a “take home” piece for further discussion.

The CTO Handbook can be a great reference for you too as you prepare to lead worship at the CTO event. The Handbook will give you insight into what coaches and their spouses will be discussing during the event and it will keep you aligned with the overall message of the event while coinciding with FCA’s Annual Ministry Theme.

articles.name: Emcee Instructions
articles.title: Emcee Instructions
articles.body:
Coaches TimeOut (CTO) is the coaches marriage ministry of FCA. CTO helps coaches and their spouses grow in their relationships with Jesus Christ and each other.

As the CTO Emcee, you play a vital role in helping coaches and their spouses in that journey.

As the emcee, you serve as the onstage facilitator during the main sessions of the event. You must ensure that the event stays on topic and on schedule. As an emcee, you should be a great communicator and highly personable. You can serve to bring continuity to the entire event and keep the main sessions running smoothly. Main sessions set the groundwork for discipleship conversations during and after the event.

As the emcee, you can shepherd coaches and their spouses through an encounter with Jesus as they move between the main sessions, discussions with each other (1-on-1), and into the context of community (Huddles).

You can help coaches and spouses win where it matters most—at home.

The outline below is intended to help you prepare to emcee at the CTO event.

FYI

Make sure to have regular discussions with your CTO Director about the overall vision for the CTO event. And ask about your specific audience, so you can prepare accordingly.

Quick Tips

  • Be available and visible. Attendees are more inclined to engage themselves when they are more familiar with you personally!
  • Make yourself available to the Huddle Leader Coordinator (HLC) in case a Huddle needs you to visit their group.
  • Organize and mobilize prayer. CTO events are spiritual battlegrounds! Enlist key people to pray for you, the worship, the speaker, the main sessions, the one-on-ones, the coaches, and their spouses.
  • Stay consistent. Review the Annual Ministry Theme resources, the Curriculum and Resources, and the CTO Handbook for contextualization.

Gospel-Centered

The Fellowship of Christian Athletes is an inter-denominational discipleship ministry inviting coaches and athletes into a personal relationship with Jesus Christ. FCA is focused on communicating the basics of the Gospel message:

  • God loves you.
  • Sin separates you from God.
  • Jesus rescues you (the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ).
  • The opportunity to trust Jesus (Will you trust Jesus?).

Visit The FOUR

Since FCA is an inter-denominational ministry, please avoid potentially divisive topics that attend denominationalism, such as baptism, communion, and spiritual gifts (such as speaking in tongues, healing, and prophecy).

Curriculum and Resources

Faith, Family, Finance

To help coaches and their spouses grow in their relationships with Jesus Christ and with each other, a CTO event will focus on three main topics:

  1. Faith
    Regardless of where coaches and spouses are on their spiritual journeys, Jesus Christ has a greater plan for their marriage. When He's at the center, everything changes in their lives.
  2. Family
    Coaches are always on the go. Pretty soon, they may realize that they spend more time in the locker room than they do at the dinner table. When the scoreboard finally turns off, a coach’s spouse and family shouldn't settle for whatever's left—they can get coach’s best.
  3. Finance
    Whether coaches make a little or a lot, a lack of intentionality can cause financial pressure sure to increase the strain on their marriage. With a foundation of Biblical principles, coaches and spouses can steward their money in healthy ways.
Your primary focus should be on faith when you emcee and speak during the main sessions. Please consult with your CTO Director if he or she would like you to address other topics.

CTO HANDBOOK

Based on FCA's annual ministry theme, the CTO Handbook guides coaches and their spouses on a journey to apply key Biblical truth to their marriage. Each lesson explores Biblical teaching on faith, family, and finance and provides couples with discussion questions to help them apply what they learned to their marriage.

CTO Handbook

Ideally, the Handbook can help create a rhythm for a coach and their spouse to have a “one-on-one” together, studying God’s Word and discussing its application to their lives, marriage, and coaching. At a CTO event, this resource may be used during these one-on-one times, at Huddles, or even as a “take home” piece for further discussion.

The CTO Handbook can be a great reference for you too as you prepare to emcee at the CTO event. The Handbook will give you insight into what coaches and their spouses will be discussing during the event and it will keep you aligned with the overall message of the event while coinciding with FCA’s Annual Ministry Theme.

articles.name: Speaker Instructions
articles.title: Speaker Instructions
articles.body:
Coaches TimeOut (CTO) is the coaches marriage ministry of FCA. CTO helps coaches and their spouses grow in their relationships with Jesus Christ and each other. CTO is also intended to catalyze E3 Discipleship.

As the CTO Speaker, you play a vital role in helping coaches and their spouses in that journey. An intimate relationship with Jesus Christ will help coaches and their spouses win where it matters most—at home.

Please familiarize yourself with the Annual Ministry Theme as well as the CTO Handbook below.

This season, your CTO is one of the hundreds of CTO events that will impact thousands of coaches and their spouses.

FYI

Make sure to have regular discussions with your CTO Director about the overall vision for the CTO event. And ask about your specific audience, so you can prepare accordingly.

Quick Tips

The most important thing to remember as you prepare your message(s) is this:

Keep your audience in mind.

Gospel-Centered

The Fellowship of Christian Athletes is an inter-denominational discipleship ministry inviting coaches and athletes into a personal relationship with Jesus Christ. FCA is focused on communicating the basics of the Gospel message:

  2. Family

articles.name: Password Reset Instructions
articles.title: Password Reset Instructions
articles.body:
Follow these instructions if you need to reset your FCA University password. (If you are a prospective staff person with FCA and need to reset your password in order to complete Tryout Training, refer to these instructions instead.)

Go to university.fca.org and click the "Forgot Password" link:

Next, enter your account email address and click the "Request reset link" button:

Check your email for an email from "FCA University." Follow the link in that email to reset your password. (If you don't see an email within a few minutes, then please check your spam folder.)

After clicking the link, enter in a new password and "Save":

Now that your password has been reset, click "Go to FCA University":

Finally, log in with your email and new password.

For additional help, please contact FCA Support Services: supportservices@fca.org | 800.289.0909

articles.name: 360 Coach Guide
articles.title: 360 Coach Guide
articles.body:
360 Coach: Website

The 360 Coach website is a simple and clear website that gives an overview of 360 Coach and encourages coaches to signup for the free online course and to download the Bible Study.

View Website

360 Coach: Online Course

The 360 Coach course is a powerful 90-minute course on FCA University. It is available for anyone to take (FCA Staff, coaches, volunteers, etc.).

The goal of the course is to help coaches get equipped through God’s Word and to learn the Bible’s clear direction for their lives.

Coaches will receive a practical framework that will help them develop a Transformational Purpose Statement, unpacking the WHY and HOW of coaching. They will get training on how to access and form a strategy for taking care of their heart, mind, and body. They will get tools that will teach them how to be a great coach so that they can reflect Jesus to their players and make a Kingdom impact in their lives.

Ideally, the course should be completed in conjunction with the 360 Coach Bible Study (see below) over a 10-week period.

View 360 Coach Online Course

360 Coach: Bible Study

The 360 Coach Bible Study is a companion resource to guide you through a biblical approach to coaching the heart, mind, and body. Each lesson explores the biblical truths outlined in 360 Coach and how they apply to your life as a coach. Use the 360 Coach Bible Study to enhance your individual, Coaches Huddle, or 1-on-1 studies.

Download the PDF version of the 360 Coach Bible Study below:

View 360 Coach Bible Study

articles.name: Inner Circle
articles.title: Inner Circle
articles.body:
Location/Facility

Coach’s office, team meeting room.

Time

15-20 minutes.

Season

Any.

Age Level

Any.

DIRECTIONS:

Athletes are conditioned to think that they are valuable because of “what they do,” instead of “who they are.” This is reinforced when fans and onlookers seek to associate with athletes who perform well and ignore those athletes who don’t. Or when fans pursue a high-performing athlete when they’re doing well and lose interest when they aren’t or when they experience a sidelining injury. Inner Circle helps athletes identify the people in their life who love and support them for “who they are” over and above “what they do.” Taken from “Identity Foreclosure” by Brett Ledbetter (https://whatdriveswinning.com/conference-videos/identity-foreclosure/).

  1. Ask each of your athletes to draw a circle on a piece of paper.
  2. Inside the circle, write the names of one to five people who they believe will love and support them no matter what.
  3. Athletes should keep the circle somewhere that it can be referred to often.

Encourage athletes to spend the majority of their discretionary time with the people inside their circle.

RESULTS:

Inner Circle creates a support system for athletes that is resilient to the ups and downs of a sports season or career. The Inner Circle helps athletes maintain a sense that they are loved apart from their performance. It will help keep performance in its proper place. It will also help athletes maintain emotional and psychological stability.

articles.name: Big-Little
articles.title: Big-Little
articles.body:
Location/Facility

Varied.

Time

Varied.

Season

Any.

Age Level

Any.

DESCRIPTION:

The idea behind Big-Little is the power of peer modeling in mentoring and role model relationships­—like a big brother or big sister to a little brother or little sister. This kind of relationship has a profound impact on the mentee and the mentor. Mentors take responsibility for the wellbeing of their mentee and themselves, often resulting in higher standards for both. Upperclassmen athletes (or “Bigs”) can be paired with underclassmen (or “Littles”), or varsity athletes can be paired with junior varsity or freshmen athletes. Pairings are best if they are within the same event or position group on the team so that Bigs and Little can have maximum interaction.

If pairing teammates together in mentor/mentee relationships is unrealistic for your team, you can create a similar effect by having upperclassmen or varsity athletes lead the underclassmen or junior varsity athletes in a team meeting or service project.

TIPS:

  • To encourage interaction, create an opportunity for the Bigs to serve the Littles. Developing relationships takes time and not everyone is comfortable with it. Creating an opportunity for the Bigs to serve the Littles will quickly break down barriers through practical action.
  • Match athletes with others who are in the same position or event group. Pair males with males and females with females.
  • Match athletes based on leadership and responsibility rather than friendship. The goal is not friendship, the goal is positive influence and leadership, although friendship will most likely be an outcome.
  • When underclassmen become upperclassmen, they become a Big and get paired with a Little from the incoming class.

RESULTS:

Big-Little creates a culture of responsibility and cooperation. It will cultivate leadership in upperclassmen and a sense of belonging and care for underclassmen. It helps pass down the habits and traditions of the team. It also delegates accountability so that you, as the coach, are not the sole disciplinarian. Teammates take responsibility for each other’s actions because they are accountable to one another.

articles.name: Character Goals
articles.title: Character Goals
articles.body:
Location/Facility

Coach’s office, team meeting room.

Time

10-15 minutes.

Season

Pre-season.

Age Level

Any.

DESCRIPTION:

Most likely, as a coach, you help your athletes set performance goals each season. These are important as first-dimension performance is necessary to winning competitions and improving as an athlete, but as a 360 Coach, you are determined to develop your athletes beyond the first dimension. That’s where Character Goals come in. As you meet with your team and your individual athletes, help them set not only performance goals, but character goals. If they desire to get faster or stronger, do they also desire to be a better servant or leader? They will train to become faster and stronger. How can they train to become a better servant?

TIPS:

  • Work together with your athletes to determine their goals rather than determining goals for them. It’s important for each athlete to have ownership in the creation of their goals.
  • Limit the number of Character Goals to three. Make them specific, measurable, attainable, relevant, and time-bound.
  • Help each athlete determine not only the goal, but steps to achieve it. For example, if an athlete sets a goal to be a better servant, they could take a step to help clean up after practice each week or encourage one teammate every day.
  • Revisit goals regularly and at the end of the season.

RESULTS:

Character Goals elevate the importance of character development on your team. Athletes will understand that character is as important as performance (hopefully even more so). As a coach, Character Goals help you hold your athletes accountable to the character attributes that they have identified as valuable, and they invite you in as an ally to their process for developing their character.

articles.name: One Word
articles.title: One Word
articles.body:
Location/Facility

Coach’s office, team meeting room.

Time

5-10 minutes per athlete.

Season

Pre-season.

Age Level

Any.

DESCRIPTION:

Based on the concept of the book “One Word” by John Gordon, Jimmy Page, and Dan Britton, the One Word drill helps athletes and coaches identify one word to define their season. This one word becomes the compass for individual actions and attitudes. Every athlete (and we encourage every coach as well) should identify their One Word for the season. You can also identify One Word for the team.

“In 1999, the authors discovered a better way to become their best and live a life of impact. Instead of creating endless goals and resolutions, they found one word that would be their driving force for the year. No goals. No wish lists. Just one word. Best of all… anyone anytime can discover their word for the year.

One Word that Will Change Your Life will inspire you to simplify your life and work by focusing on just one word for this year. That’s right! One Word creates clarity, power, passion, and life-change. The simple power of One Word is that it impacts all six dimensions of your life – mental, physical, emotional, relational, spiritual, and financial. Simply put, One Word sticks. There is a word meant for you and when you find it, live it, and share it, your life will become more rewarding and exciting than ever.

Join thousands of people and hundreds of schools, businesses, churches, and sports teams who have found their one word… and discover how to harness the transformational power of One Word” (https://jongordon.com/books/one-word/).

TIPS:

  • Coach, you might want to know the One Word for each of your athletes, but that’s a lot of words to know. It might be wise to make your assistant/position coaches responsible for knowing each One Word for each of their athletes.
  • For each person, One Word should encompass who they are, not just what they do. It should describe the type of person they aspire to be in their actions and attitude.
  • Revisit the One Word often with your athletes and coaches, especially during challenging moments when attitudes are tested.
  • You could conduct an individual meeting with each athlete to determine their One Word, or you could make it an exercise during a team meeting and ask each athlete to submit their One Word to their position coach following the meeting.

RESULTS:

The One Word drill encourages each athlete and coach to think about the bigger picture and maintain perspective throughout the season. It helps coaches and athletes think about who they are becoming over what they are doing. It sustains focus during the ups and downs of the season and orients actions and attitudes toward a greater goal or theme.

articles.name: Cross the Line
articles.title: Cross the Line
articles.body:
Location/Facility

Practice field, court, pool lane, anywhere you can cross a line.

Time

15-20 minutes.

Season

Any, depending on the questions asked.

Age Level

Pre-season/off-season training/team building.

DIRECTIONS:

The coach should be the facilitator for this drill.

  1. Have everyone line up behind a line, rope, or tape, facing the same direction.
  2. Explain that you’re going to read a series of statements. If the statement is true for them, they should step across the line, turn around and look at the team. Pause for a couple of seconds so teammates can see each other, and then have them walk back to their place after being instructed by you. Say, “Go ahead and return to the other side.”
  3. Warn them that some questions are personal. It’s perfectly alright if someone isn’t comfortable crossing the line and everyone has the option to choose when they cross the line.
  4. The activity is done in silence. There are no judgments on anyone for crossing or not crossing the line. If you know something is true for someone and they choose not to cross (or vice versa), respect their privacy and don’t call them out.
  5. Remind players of the confidentiality guideline. We will talk about the activity at the end, so if something makes you think or you have a question, save it until the end so we can address it.

SAMPLE STATEMENTS:

Sample statements are listed below. They start off light-hearted and fun to get things going, and then progress to more personal and potentially challenging topics. Add questions that are more specific to your team.
Cross the line if you:

  • Like ice cream.
  • Think Spring is the best season.
  • Love snow.
  • Are an only child.
  • Like rap music.
  • Have ever been in a fight.
  • Have ever been bullied.
  • Find it hard to say “no” to your friends.
  • Have ever felt unsafe at school.
  • Have ever been suspended or expelled.
  • Consider yourself a leader.
  • Do some form of creative art (drawing, writing, music, dance, theatre, etc.).
  • Play a musical instrument.
  • Have ever seen a family member excessively use drugs or alcohol.
  • Have parents who are divorced.
  • Had an argument with your parents in the last week.
  • Have been hurt by a family member.
  • Have gotten in a physical fight with a sibling.
  • Have a family member who died.
  • Have had a parent die.
  • Have a family member who’s been in jail.
  • Have a parent who’s been unemployed.
  • Heard gun shots in your neighborhood.
  • Have cried in the last month.
  • Are worried about your future.
  • Are adopted.
  • Feel like your family is proud of you.
  • Have an adult in your life that you really trust.
  • Have been treated unfairly because of your… (ask a separate question for each category):
    • race
    • socioeconomic status
    • gender
    • age
  • Have been made fun of for your weight.
  • Have a significant other.
  • Have done something in the last month that you regret.
  • Find it difficult to trust others.
  • Wish you were closer to your teammates.
  • Feel our team isn’t unified.
  • Want to be a better friend.
  • Find it difficult to stand up for what you believe in.
  • Wish God gave you different gifts and talents.
  • Would not change anything about yourself even if you could.
  • Stayed in a relationship that you knew wasn’t working for you.
  • Lied to someone you were dating.
  • Regretted doing something to someone you dated.

AFTER DRILL:

After the drill, process the following questions with your team. Follow-up conversations can happen at your discretion.

  • Was there anything you liked or hated about this teambuilder?
  • Were there things you were surprised to see how many people crossed for? Or how few?
  • Did you learn anything about one of your teammates you didn’t already know?
  • Which ones were hard to admit to for you? Which were easy?
  • How do you feel about the team after this exercise?

RESULTS:

This drill will give your team an opportunity to learn things about each other that they might not otherwise learn. It will cultivate empathy among teammates as they learn hard things that others have faced. It will build bonds among those with shared experiences. It will encourage a safe atmosphere for teammates to know and be known by each other. Because of the nature of this drill, as the coach, it’s important to set a tone of safety and understanding.

articles.name: Spotlight Drill
articles.title: Spotlight Drill
articles.body:
Location/Facility

Circle up after a practice or game.

Time

2-5 minutes after practice and/or games.

Season

Any.

Age Level

Any.

DESCRIPTION:

This drill is typically best positioned after practice but could be done any time your athletes are together. Select one athlete from the team to be in the “spotlight”. Ask the other members of the team to say the person’s name and spotlight one thing that they appreciate about her/him. Comments should spotlight a specific action or behavior that demonstrates a positive character attribute or value that contributes to the team. The more specific the statement, the more powerful the effect.

TIPS:

  • Make sure the statement is directed to the person and not about the person.
  • Coach your athletes to look each other in the eye when delivering and receiving the statements.
  • Coach the person in the spotlight to receive the words of affirmation and not deflect them.
  • Coach athletes to be specific. When an athlete says, for example, “Jill, I appreciate the way you help people.” Ask them to be specific. Say instead, “Jill, I appreciate the ride home that you’ve given me when my mom wasn’t able to pick me up.”
  • Encourage your athletes to spotlight things that are character-based above things that are performance-based.

RESULTS:

This drill creates a culture of appreciation and value among teammates. When most athletes feel that their value and contribution is based on their performance, this drill teaches athletes to value one another based on their character and virtue. This is especially impactful for those who don’t get a lot of playing time. Athletes are more likely to commit to a team where they feel like they are valued and are making a meaningful contribution to others.

articles.name: Creating a Team FCA Profile
articles.title: Creating a Team FCA Profile
articles.body:
To apply for an open staff position with FCA you must create a profile in the Team FCA portal.

Team FCA Portal

FYI

You must have a profile to complete the application process. Your profile will allow you to apply for more than 1 job quickly and easily.

To create a profile, simply begin the application process for a posted job. You will be prompted to create your account. Simply provide your email address and create a password, then click "Create Account":

Attention

Use an email address you have easy and frequent access to! There are many email notifications that guide you through the hiring process. Avoid using school or former job emails addresses that you may lose access to.

After creating your profile, you can complete the initial part of any job posting.

If you progress through the hiring process, you will be prompted at various steps to log into your profile and complete additional steps in the application.

To log back into the Team FCA portal, simply click " Sign In" in the upper right-hand corner and provide the login credentials you had previously created:

articles.name: Image Map Guide
articles.title: Image Map Guide
articles.body:
If you would like to include a clickable map with a photo of your area, this guide will help you create such a image with clickable links on your website.

Prerequisites:

  • An image in .png or .jpg format
  • Links to the sites that you would like to connect

1) In the editor of your website, go to Content --> Site Content --> Manage Images. If you have not upload your image to your site yet, upload it now by click Upload Images.

2) Grab the URL for the image on your site by first clicking on the image, then right-clicking on the title of the file, and select the following based on your browser:

  • Chrome = "Copy link address"
  • Firefox = "Copy link location"
  • Safari = "Copy link"

3) Go to https://www.image-map.net/

4) Click on "Load image from my website"

5) Paste in the link to your image (from step 2) and click "Continue"

6) You should now see your image with some options to begin mapping the image with the link settings below.

  • Shape = Select the appropriate shape you need to make a clickable area on your image (Rectangle, Poly, Circle). Poly allows you to click the outlines of a your map for an area, and provides the most flexibility.
  • Link = the link you want the area of the map to go to
  • Target:
    • "---" = opens the link in the same window
    • "_blank = opens the link in a new window. Most popular if you don't want the user to lose your website in their tabs of the browser they are using.
    • "_parent", "_self", "_top" = not used in modern web design and recommended not to select these options.

7) Begin tracing the area that you need to be linked on the image above. If you selected "Poly" begin tracing the area by clicking where you need the points to be on. You should see a grey box connect your lines. That is clickable area you are setting up.

8) If you need more areas, click on "Add New Area" and redo steps 6 and 7 until you have all places mapped out. Be sure to select the "Active" radio button for the row that you are working on, so that you don't mess up your previous area. Once you have finished, click on "Show Me the Code!"

9) Highlight and copy the code that is in the popup textbox.

10) Go back to your website, and place an HTML Widget on your page.

11) Paste in the code and click "Update"

12) This will place your image with the links on your site. To test the image with the links, you can use the "Preview" mode to see if it is working correctly. If working correctly, feel free to Republish your new mapped Image to your site.

If you have any issues mapping your image, feel free to reach out to the Support Center (800) 289-0909 or email the WebFCA Support team at webfca@fca.org

articles.name: Parent Technical Support Index
articles.title: Parent Technical Support Index
articles.body:
The FCA Sports management tool, Sports Connect, maintains helpful instructions for all league and club management tasks in their help center. Below is a short index of topics that cover common tasks for parents whose children are involved in an FCA Sports program:

  • Getting Started (i.e., Registration, refunds, cancellations, etc.)
  • FAQs
  • Navigating my Account

FYI

All Sports Connect related questions can also be directed to the Sports Connect help desk.

Or call 1-866-258-3303.

articles.name: Site Overview and Stats Guide
articles.title: Site Overview and Stats Guide
articles.body:
Site Overview is your new site management tool, designed to tell each site’s story at a glance. This dashboard helps you to quickly see your site's visits, engagements, and form responses. Use this guide to help you understand this new site management tool.

Download Form Responses

1) If you have multiple forms on your site, select the appropriate form to see their responses in the dropdown.

2) Click on "Export to CSV" to download the responses into Microsoft Excel

Stats Overview

To get a full understanding of your site, use this guide to help you understand the various stat panels to help you make sense of the performance of your website. To see the full stats of your website, first click on "Open Full Stats" in the Site Overview Dashboard.

1) For each Select Time Frame for the stats that you are looking for

2) Overview Panel

  • Gives key information about interaction with your website including site traffic based on device over a set time period.

3) Content Panel

  • A breakdown of statistics for each page of your site.

4) Engagement Panel

  • A deep view into the actions that visitors are taking on this website.

5) Traffic Sources Panel

  • Learn the source of visitors that come to this site. This is valuable to understand how users get to your website.

6) Browsers & OSs Panel

  • Discover what type of devices your visitors are using by checking out what browser and operating system they're on.

7) Geolocation Panel

  • Find out the location data of users visiting your website.

8) Download Form Responses

  • Download the submission data into an Excel spreadsheet by selecting the appropriate form and clicking on the download icon.

articles.name: Backup and Restore Sites
articles.title: Backup and Restore Sites
articles.body:
Backups save images, files, content—everything on the site is backed up except eCommerce data.

You can save copies of your site called backups. Backups allow you to restore to them at any point in time. While our system automatically creates backups each time a site is published, it is recommended to create manual backups of your site periodically instead of relying on the automated backup system in place.

In general, it is a good idea to create a backup:

  • After you are satisfied with the look of your site.
  • After you have finished a hard-to-do task (such as importing several pictures, coding, and so on).
  • Before adding any custom code or doing any big changes to the site.

There is a limit of 30 automated backups and 10 manual backups that can be stored. New manual backups override earlier manual backups, thus your earliest manual backup is deleted when you create a new one. This is also the case for automatic backups; the earliest automatic backup is deleted when a new one is created. Automated backups get deleted out of the backup list often because of how it is structured. If you want to keep an automated backup, simply restore to an automated backup, and then create a manual backup from that.

Automated Backups

Automated backups are created after:

  • Republishing
  • Adding or removing the multi-language feature
  • Restoring a backup
  • Deleting a site
  • Accessing developer mode
  • Resetting a site
  • Changing a site layout
  • Upgrading a store to the new layout available 1/27/2019

Create Manual Backups

  1. In left panel, click Settings, and then click Backup Site.
  2. Type a New Version Name for the backup.
  3. Click Save.

Restore From Backup

  1. In left panel, click Settings, and then click Backup Site.
  2. Click the Restore link next to the backup you want to restore.
  3. Type a name for your backup.

Delete Backups

  1. In the left panel, click Site Settings, and then click Backup Site.
  2. Click the Delete link next to the backup you want to delete.

Blog Backups

Blog content is backed up when your site is backed up. If you restore a backup, the blog will be the one in the restored version of the site. Note that you will not see the restored version until the restored site is published. Until then, the run time and editor will look different, as with any site backup.

A post on a non-published restored version of a blog will not be live until the restored site is published (unlike with regular posts, which do not require the site to be published again for the post to go live).

As always, a new backup version is automatically created when restoring a back up version.

articles.name: Site Icons
articles.title: Site Icons
articles.body:
Home screen icons, share images, favicons, and startup images allow you to customize, brand, and polish your user's experience on the site, as well as make it easier for a visitor to pick your site out from a bunch of tabs or links on their home screen. If you are confused about what icons, share images, favicons, or start-up images are, please see below.

Note

Be sure to republish your site after adding or changing any of the site icons. You may need to refresh the page or clear the cache in order to see the icon changes.

To edit your site icons:

  1. In the left panel, click Settings, and then click Site Icons.
  2. Click +Image next to the icon you want to edit.

Favicon

Your favicon appears next to your page title in the tab of your browser. It allows users to identify and navigate to your site easily, even if they have multiple tabs open.

Note

Allowed resolution sizes for favicons are (px): 24 x 24, 36 x 36, 48 x 48.

Social Networks Image (Open Graph)

This is the image designated to display on Facebook, Twitter, and other social networks when sharing your site.

Home Screen Icon (Mobile & Tablet)

Home screen icons are a mobile phone version of bookmarks, and appear on iPhone and iPad devices when your site is bookmarked on the home screen. Users are able to save a home screen icon on their phone's home screen as if the site were an app. Clicking this icon on mobile devices brings users to your site.

This image should be a square and at least 57 x 57 pixels in size.

articles.name: URL Redirects
articles.title: URL Redirects
articles.body:
The URL Redirect feature lets you redirect specific URLs on your site to specific pages. Replacing your site with a new responsive site temporarily affects your search engine rankings while Google and other search engines adjust to the changes. Use the URL redirect feature to redirect your old page URLs to their new responsive site URLs and keep search engines happy.

To set up a URL redirect:

  1. In the left panel, click Settings, and then click URL Redirect.
  2. Click Add new redirect to manually create a URL redirect.
  3. Type the original URL to be redirected in the Old page name field. For example, if your site originally had a page yoursite.com/my-page.html, type /my-page.html.
  4. Select the redirect Destination page. You can choose to use a page from your new site or an external URL.
  5. To edit or delete your custom URL redirects, click Edit or Delete in the redirect menu.

URL Redirects are very useful if you have multiple sites that you want forwarded to your responsive site. Redirects are also important when you are switching domains or moving specific pages to a new domain. While URL redirects are very beneficial in these cases, they can also cause visitors confusion to which site they are visiting. When using redirects, make sure the visitor understands that they will be redirected so they do not mistake it for phishing attacks.

Redirect with URL Parameters

Specific URL redirects do not work on URL parameters. A URL parameter is anything that comes after the ? or # in the URL. Website Builder treats any URL with a URL parameter as the same URL.

That is, this URL: http://www.sitename.com/page1 is the same as this URL: http://www.sitename.com/page1?url_referrer=blank.

If you have a 301 redirect set up on page 1, it will also redirect any /page1 URL with a URL parameter. If you need a specific URL redirect for a URL parameter, please check with your DNS host to see if this is something they can set up for you.

articles.name: Site SEO Settings
articles.title: Site SEO Settings
articles.body:
Search engine optimization (SEO) is the process of improving the visibility of a website or a web page in a search engine's un-paid search results. Sometimes this is called "natural" or "organic" traffic. The better your site's SEO, the more likely it is to rank well in search results.

Improve your site’s visibility to search engines and boost organic traffic by filling in the site’s SEO fields. This makes it easier for search engines to know what your sites are about so they can be displayed higher on search result pages (SERPs).

To edit your overall site's SEO:

In the left panel, click Settings, and then click SEO. You have the following options:

  • Site Title. This is displayed by search engines in their preview of your site. It is important for both SEO and social sharing. Try to limit this to 60 characters.
  • Site Description. Give an overview of the content and information the site provides. Search engines uses this to display as a description for the site. Try to limit to 160 characters.
    Note It is best practice for each page of your site to have a unique title and description. For this reason, we recommend entering your page title and description individually in the Page Settings menu.
  • Site Keywords. These are terms related to your site (for example, pizza delivery, for a pizza shop). Separate keywords with a comma.
    Note Search engines no longer use keywords to impact website ratings. All keyword usage should be considered optional.

Site Title

This is the most important tag for your site SEO. This tag tells people and search engines what your page is about. The site title appears in the title bar of the web browser and in search engine results.

The title tag should contain important keywords to help search engines determine what your page is about. Also, real people will be reading the site title when deciding whether or not to click on a search result, so make sure to make it people-friendly.

This field can be connected to the Content Library via Connected Data. For more information, see Connected Data.

Site Description

The site description may be used by search engines when they display the content of your site on their results page. So, while this field is not important for search engine rankings, it is very important for compelling users to click through to the site.

This description is your opportunity to advertise site content to people and let them know exactly whether your page contains the information they are looking for. Create a concise and compelling description that people will want to click on.

This field can be connected to the Content Library via Connected Data. For more information, see Connected Data.

Site Keywords

Major search engines do not use keywords to determine your site's ranking. In fact, on Bing.com, your site can be penalized for having overly "spammy" keywords. However, some smaller free directories and search engines may still use them to help categorize sites. All keyword usage should be considered optional.

Lazy Loading and Fonts

Lazy loading of images and widgets improves page speed by loading images and widgets when they become visible on scroll. You can also enable an option to display default system fonts so visitors can start reading instantly while site fonts are loading.

Note

Site may blink when fonts are loaded.

To enable lazy loading and fonts, click the Lazy loading of images & widgets and Show default system fonts while custom fonts are loading toggles.

articles.name: Progressive Web App (PWA)
articles.title: Progressive Web App (PWA)
articles.body:
A Progressive Web App (PWA) is a web application that enables you to deliver a native app-like experience to users on mobile so that they have a fast, smooth, and engaging experience with your site.

PWAs load instantly, respond quickly to user interactions, and feel like a natural app. They do not need an app store for installation and enable users to re-enter the site instantly, even when a device is offline.

Note
  • PWAs are supported on Chrome for Android. If PWA is enabled, the browser automatically asks the user if they want to add the site in the home screen.
  • You can also add PWA on IOS devices running version 11.3. However, since the "add to screen" automatic message by the browser is not yet available, users will have to add PWA manually. For more information on how to do this, visit Apple Support.

To activate PWA feature:

  1. In the left panel, click Settings, and then click Progressive Web App (PWA).
  2. Click the Activate Progressive Web App toggle. Once PWA has been activated for a site, you can configure the following settings:
    • Name. This is the name displayed on the splash screen and the “Add to homescreen message.” It can be up to 60 characters long.
    • Short Name. This is the name used when there is not enough space to display the full name; for example, on the mobile homescreen. This name can be up to 60 characters, but we suggest that you make it as short as you can.
    • Icon. This is the icon that appears on the mobile homescreen and the splash screen when the app is loading. It must be at least 192 pixels width and in .png format.
    • Splash screen background color. This is the color that appears while the site is loading.

When a user accesses a site enabled with PWA from their mobile device, they will see an Add to Home screen button displayed below the site. Once added to the home screen, the icon will be displayed on their device for quick access to the site (similar to an app).

The user can now re-enter the site using this icon, even if the device is offline.

articles.name: Site Settings
articles.title: Site Settings
articles.body:
Most of the general options for your site are available in the Settings tab in the left panel. From here, you can backup your site, edit your site's SEO and more.

Site Icons

Home screen icons, share icons, favicons, and startup images allow you to customize, brand, and polish your user's experience on the site and make it easier for a user to pick your site out from a bunch of tabs or links on their home screen. For more information, see Site Icons.

Site URL

The site URL is how visitors see your site. You are assigned a default, public URL until you decide to add your own domain.

SEO

SEO is what gets your site to first page on Google. You can change settings here to help get a little boost on search engine optimization. For more information about SEO, see Introduction to SEO.

These settings are different from individual page SEO settings. Page SEO overrides global SEO changes made in site settings. For more information, see Site SEO Settings.

URL Redirect

The URL Redirect feature lets you redirect specific URLs on your site to specific pages. For more information, see URL Redirects.

Header HTML

The header HTML is a location for you to add your custom scripts, meta tags and other pieces of code which belong in the <head> section of your site. For more information, see Header HTML.

Note

Only certain types of code can be placed in the header HTML. Incorrect code will be removed.

Backup site

Create a backup of your site that you can restore to at anytime. This allows you to revert changes or restore back from critical mistakes done on the site.

To create a backup, in the left panel, click Settings, and then click Backup Site. Type a new version name for the backup and click Save.

For more information about site backups, Backup Sites.

Site Language

If you are building a site in a language other than English, the Site Language feature allows you to translate certain elements into your target language. For more information, see Set a Default Site Language.

Privacy Settings

With the help of the Privacy Settings page, you can ensure that your users are well-informed about the type of data and cookies information collected when they visit your page. The main tools you can enable in Privacy Settings are the privacy page and cookie notification. For more information, see Privacy Settings.

404 Page

Customize the 404 page for your site. The 404 page is used in case an invalid or unreachable URL is entered as an internal link of the site domain; that is, in lieu of any page that can not be rendered. If a site visitor tries to access a non-existent page on your site, they will see your customized 404 page as opposed to the generic 404 page that we provide by default.

To customize the 404 page:

  1. In the left panel, click Settings, and then click 404 page.
  2. Click Customize page. The 404 page will not display under the Pages menu or the Pages drop-down at the top left of the editor.
  3. Editing the 404 page is similar to editing any other page on your site. You can drag in any widget or section, and edit content and design. However, to navigate back to the rest of your site, you need to go back to the Home page from the pages drop-down at the top of the page.
  4. To preview your 404 page, click Preview page. You can always revert back to the original 404 page by clicking Revert to original page.
Note
  • For multi-language sites, the 404 page will be the same in all languages and can only be customized in the default site language.

articles.name: Header HTML
articles.title: Header HTML
articles.body:
The Header HTML feature allows you to add HTML, CSS, or JavaScript to the header section of your site. Quickly add tracking code, JavaScript to your site's <head> section through a simple user interface.

  • Only use code you are familiar with and from a source that you trust.
  • It is a good idea to use the Site Backup Feature before adding custom code.
  • This feature gives you access to the <head> HTML section, <body end> HTML section, and allows you to configure quick navigation.

Note

We cannot assist in troubleshooting custom code.

Access the Header HTML Section

  1. In the left panel, click Settings.
  2. Click Head HTML.
    • Head HTML. Add content to the head section field.
    • Body End HTML. Add custom HTML/Javascript just before the closing tag of the </body> element for each page in the site.
  3. Click Save.

articles.name: Privacy Settings
articles.title: Privacy Settings
articles.body:
With the help of the Privacy Settings page, you can ensure that your users are well-informed about the type of data and cookies information collected when they visit your page. The main tools you can enable in Privacy Settings are the privacy page and cookie notification.

Privacy Page

A privacy page helps to make sure that site visitors understand what kind of information is being collected, how it is done, safeguarded, what the rights of the user and site owner are, and more.

To add or update the privacy page on your site:

  1. In the left panel, click Settings, and then click Privacy Settings.
  2. Click the Customize Privacy Page toggle.
  3. This will prompt you to customize the text of the Privacy page. If your site did not have a privacy page until now, one will be automatically created. If you are ready to change the default text of the page, click Customize.
  4. For your convenience, all elements that need customization are highlighted in red.
  5. Make the necessary changes and when you republish the site, the privacy page will be added or updated.
  6. Any additional changes can be made at any time by clicking Customize.
  7. The default link for the privacy page is [yourdomain]/privacy, but can be changed to any custom link in Privacy page URLfield.
Note

To add this link to your site navigation, add a new page, set the new page to an external URL and paste the privacy page URL in the field provided. For more information, see Pages and Popups.

  1. To revert the changes you have made on the default privacy page, click Reset to original page.

Cookie Notifications

Some countries, particularly those in the EU, require websites to notify visitors that they use cookies. This ensures your site is compliant with local law and can be polite to security-minded visitors.

To enable Cookie Notifications on your site:

  1. In the left panel, click Settings, and then click Privacy Settings.
  2. Click the Activate cookie notification toggle.
    Every visitor to your site will see a notification that the information about their site visit is collected in the form of cookies.
  3. Click Preview notification in order to preview the cookie notification on all devices. The cookie notification appears as a top bar above the content of your site.
  4. You can choose to use default text for the cookie notification or customize it. The text field is free text and you may add any text or links to it.

Tracking & Site Cookies

Disable the use of cookies sent to site visitors by the site. This disables platform analytics, including Google Analytics and the default platform analytics, and also prevents personalization rules from adding cookies.

Note

Third-party services (or widgets that include them) such as Facebook and Twitter still set cookies, as they are required to load the widgets.

To disable the use of cookies, in the left panel click Settings, click Privacy Settings, and then click the Disable Tracking Cookies toggle.

Warning

Disabling tracking cookies will stop stats collection for your site, as well as any tracking code you have added (for example, Facebook tracking pixels).

Note

Check the legal requirements in the country of your target audience regarding cookie notifications, the form of the notification, and relevant links to add to the text of the notification.

articles.name: Facebook Feeds
articles.title: Facebook Feeds
articles.body:
A Facebook feed is a great way to give your visitors a glimpse into your Facebook community, invite them to join your followers or prompt them to contact you via Facebook Messenger.

In order for your page’s feed to be displayed, the page must not be restricted. Any restriction, for example, login required, age or location, will prevent the page from being displayed in the widget. To learn more about Facebook page restrictions, see Facebook’s guide.

To add the widget:

  1. In the left panel, click Widgets.
  2. Click and drag the Facebook Feed widget into your site.

To learn more about adding widgets to your site, see Add Widgets.

Content Editor

To access the content editor:

  1. Right-click the widget, and click Edit Content.
  2. Type the Facebook Page URL in the field provided. You do not have to paste the entire link (for example, https://www.facebook.com/facebook). The page ID is enough (for example, facebook).
  3. Click to enable a Large Header, Cover photo, and Friend's faces.
  4. Select which tabs you want to display: Timeline, Events, Messages.

Design Editor

To access the design editor, right-click the widget, and click Edit Design. Click to change the width and height of the Facebook Feed.

For information about design options that are not specific to this widget (for example, layout, style, or spacing), see Widget Design.

articles.name: Blog Settings
articles.title: Blog Settings
articles.body:
Change the thumbnail, name, title, description, and backups for your blog. You can also find your blog's RSS feed URL here. Adding a blog name, title, description and URL are important because they help with your blog SEO. The more keywords used in the name, title, and description, the more relevance your blog will have on search engines. For example, using the term "food blog" in your blog name, title, description and URL helps search engines find your blog when users search for the term "food blog."

In the left panel, click Blog, and then click Blog Settings. You have the following options:

  • Blog name. This name displays on the sites as the title of the All Posts widget.
  • RSS Feed Details. This information appears in RSS feeds.
    • Blog Title. Type a title for the blog.
    • Blog Description. Describe what your blog is about so visitors know what to expect.
  • Blog Thumbnail. Click +Image to select or upload a thumbnail for the blog.
  • RSS feed URL. The RSS feed URL is auto-generated when your site is published.

articles.name: Add Widgets
articles.title: Add Widgets
articles.body:
To add widgets to a site, drag and drop them from the left panel or from the context menu.

Add Widgets from the Widgets Panel

To add widgets from the left panel:

  1. In the left panel, click Widgets to open the widgets panel. Search for widgets by browsing through the list or using the widget search bar.
  2. Click and drag the widget to the location you want in the site. A blue placement indicator appears.
  3. The widget appears in the location shown by the blue placement indicator.

Add Widgets with the Context Menu

To add widgets using the context menu:

  1. Right-click any widget and select Add widget below in the context menu to add widgets immediately below the current widget.
  2. If you are right-clicking on a column, click + Add, and select Add widget below.
  3. Select from the four most popular widgets or click More widgets to open the full menu. After adding the widget, you can edit it as usual.

When dragging-and-dropping a widget to your site, note the text in the blue placement indicator:

  • Insert here. The widget will be added to an existing row or column.
  • Insert in new row. A new row will be added and the widget will be placed inside of it.
  • Insert in new column. A new column will be added and the widget will be placed inside of it.

To learn more about editing widgets on your site, see Edit Widgets.

articles.name: Show Hidden Elements
articles.title: Show Hidden Elements
articles.body:
At any time, you can see all of the hidden widgets on a page by clicking the Unhide Elements () icon at the bottom of the left panel under Settings.

Note

The Unhide Elements icon only displays if you have hidden elements in your site.

To unhide elements:

  1. Click the hidden element in the editor.
  2. In the left panel, click Unhide Element ().
    Hidden elements are now displayed.
  3. Once hidden elements are displayed, to restore them, click on the element, and then click Yes on the message.
Note

If you are using the unhide element feature and it does not reveal an image on your site, your image might need to be added and uploaded.

articles.name: Add a Page
articles.title: Add a Page
articles.body:
In the left panel, click the "Pages" tab, then click the " New Page" button.

Next, select a page type. "Blank Page" is the most common page type to build upon using the FCA Sections.

Give a "Page Name" to your new page, and a URL, if necessary.

Click " Add Page" to get started creating your new page.
